text: The surgeon begins the procedure with access to the abdominal cavity, performing adhesiolysis using bipolar energy to divide visible adhesions.

The surgeon carefully dissects small bowel adhesions using diathermy, revealing the rectal stump marked by non-absorbable stitches from a previous operation. An assistant introduces a probe transanally to aid in identifying the stump and provide counter traction, while the surgeon takes care to avoid nerve injuries.

The surgeon performs intramesorectal dissection, highlighting the rectal stump. The dissection is complete when the rectum is visibly mobilized, reducing nerve damage risk and preparing for safe anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis on the anterior part of the rectal stump to avoid posterior rectal dissection, while the colostomy is visibly closed.
Indocyanine green (ICG) is applied to the bowel to assess perfusion and identify the optimal site for colon transection. The marked areas of the bowel are visible under fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon applies diluted iodine povidone solution to the opened colon to detect any inadvertent bowel injury. A purse string suture is performed around the opening in the colon.

The surgeon places the anvil and introduces the bowel into the abdominal cavity. A gel point is positioned at the stoma site, and an additional trocar is inserted to facilitate the next surgical steps.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure to reduce tension and divides the inferior mesenteric vein. An end-to-end colorectal anastomosis is performed, with indocyanine green (ICG) used to confirm adequate perfusion at both ends of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs intraoperative endoscopy, showing the elite test results as negative. A drain is placed in the pelvis, and the final appearance of the surgical site is displayed.
